content:
# Romans Chapter 9 — The EMET Bible (Literal)
**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus (א), 4th century CE | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/ROM_009_page_001.jpg

1. Truth I speak in Christos, I do not lie, the conscience of me bearing witness with me in [the] Holy Spirit,

2. that grief is to me great, and unceasing pain in the heart of me.

3. For I was wishing I myself to be accursed from the Christos for the sake of the brothers of me, the kinsmen of me according to [the] flesh,

4. who are Israelites, whose [is] the adoption and the glory and the covenants and the giving of the law and the worship and the promises,

5. whose [are] the fathers, and from whom [is] the Christos according to [the] flesh, the [one] being over all, God blessed into the ages. Amen.

6. But [it is] not as though the word of the God has failed. For not all the [ones] from Israel, these [are] Israel.

7. Nor because they are seed of Abraham [are they] all children; but, "In Isaak shall seed be called to you."

8. That is, not the children of the flesh, these [are] children of the God; but the children of the promise are reckoned for seed.

9. For of promise [is] this word: "At this time I will come, and there will be to Sarah a son."

10. And not only [this], but also Rebekka, having conception from one — Isaak the father of us —

11. for [the children] not yet having been born, nor having practiced anything good or evil — that the purpose of the God according to election might remain, not from works but from the [one] calling —

12. it was said to her, "The greater will serve the lesser,"

13. just as it has been written, "Iakob I loved, but Esau I hated."

14. What then shall we say? [Is there] unrighteousness with the God? May it never be!

15. For to Moses he says, "I will have mercy on whom I have mercy, and I will have compassion on whom I have compassion."

16. So then [it is] not of the [one] willing nor of the [one] running, but of the God having mercy.

17. For the writing says to Pharaoh, "For this very [purpose] I raised you up, that I might show in you the power of me, and that the name of me might be proclaimed in all the earth."

18. So then, on whom he wills he has mercy, and whom he wills he hardens.

19. You will say to me then, "Why does he still find fault? For who has resisted the will of him?"

20. O human, but who are you, the [one] answering back to the God? Will the [thing] formed say to the [one] having formed [it], "Why did you make me thus?"

21. Or does not the potter have authority over the clay, from the same lump to make one vessel for honor and another for dishonor?

22. But if the God, willing to show the wrath and to make known the power of him, bore with much patience vessels of wrath having been prepared for destruction,

23. and [this] that he might make known the riches of the glory of him upon vessels of mercy, which he prepared beforehand for glory —

24. whom he also called, us, not only from [the] Ioudaioi but also from [the] nations?

25. As also in Hosea he says, "I will call the not-my-people, my people, and the not-beloved, beloved."

26. "And it will be in the place where it was said to them, 'You [are] not my people,' there they will be called sons of [the] living God."

27. And Esaias cries out concerning Israel, "Though the number of the sons of Israel be as the sand of the sea, the remnant will be saved.

28. For [the] Lord will execute the word of him upon the earth, completing and cutting [it] short."

29. And just as Esaias foretold, "If [the] Lord of Sabaoth had not left us a seed, we would have become like Sodoma and been made like Gomorra."

30. What then shall we say? That nations, the [ones] not pursuing righteousness, attained righteousness — but [the] righteousness from faith;

31. but Israel, pursuing [the] law of righteousness, did not arrive at [the] law.

32. Why? Because [it was] not from faith but as from works. They stumbled on the stone of the stumbling,

33. just as it has been written, "Behold, I place in Sion a stone of stumbling and a rock of offense, and the [one] believing upon him will not be put to shame."

---
## EMET's Textual Notes

1. **v.3 — "Accursed" (ἀνάθεμα):** Paul would accept his own damnation if it would save Israel. This echoes Moses' intercession (Exodus 32:32). The emotional intensity is extraordinary — Paul's theology of grace does not diminish his agonized love for his people.

2. **v.5 — Christological doxology:** "The one being over all, God blessed forever." The punctuation determines the theology. If a full stop comes after "flesh," then "God blessed forever" is an independent doxology. If not, Paul calls Christ "God over all." Sinaiticus has no punctuation, leaving the ambiguity unresolved. Most modern translations read it as applying to Christ.

3. **v.13 — "Esau I hated":** From Malachi 1:2-3. The Hebrew "hate" (שנא) here means "loved less" or "not chosen" — a comparative, not an absolute. Paul applies this to God's sovereign election of Jacob/Israel over Esau/Edom.

4. **v.20 — Isaiah 29:16 / 45:9 echo:** The potter/clay metaphor. Paul's point is not that God is arbitrary but that God is sovereign. The creature has no standing to interrogate the Creator's purposes.

5. **v.33 — Composite quotation:** Paul merges Isaiah 28:16 ("a stone in Zion... whoever believes will not be put to shame") with Isaiah 8:14 ("a stone of stumbling and rock of offense"). The stone is Christ — simultaneously foundation and obstacle.

— EMET, AI Translator
